mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 7 Series 728i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 728i engine typically costs £3,200–£6,500 supply only depending on the generation, with the E38's M52B28 sitting at £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends on which generation you're asking about. The M30 'big six' in the E23 and E32 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200,000+ miles is routine with proper maintenance. The M52 in the E38 is generally reliable, but it has well-documented issues: VANOS seal failure, cooling system component failure, and oil leaks. Early M52s also suffered from Nikasil cylinder liner issues. Across all, regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 728i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M30 head gasket failure and oil leaks (E23/E32), M52 VANOS failure (E38), cooling system failures (all generations), and M52 Nikasil cylinder liner issues (early E38). See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 728i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-maintained E38 728i (values £4,000–£12,000+ depending on condition), a £4,000–£5,000 reconditioned engine is 30–60% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. For an E23 or E32, values can vary significantly based on condition and rarity. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 728i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. As a rule of thumb: E23 (1977–1986) and E32 (1986–1992) = M30B28; E38 (1995–2001) = M52B28. Check your VIN plate and vehicle documentation for confirmation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M52 VANOS, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: M52 VANOS failure is always repairable without replacing the engine. VANOS seal replacement costs £300–£800 and restores lost low-end torque and performance. However, if VANOS failure has led to other issues, more extensive work may be required. At the first sign of hesitation or loss of power, have the VANOS system inspected.

eraMap.title: The 7 SERIES 728i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Original 728i — cast-iron M30 'big six'; 170–184 bhp; legendary durability but head gasket and oil leaks are age-related watchpoints
Second-generation M30 — 184 bhp; same robust architecture as E23; cooling system and timing chain tensioner need attention
Modern M52 — 190–193 bhp, 24-valve, aluminium block; VANOS seals, cooling system and oil leaks are the key watchpoints

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: Budget priority; finding a good donor M30/M52 is becoming harder
Most owners — best cost/warranty balance for this classic engine
Long-term ownership; full factory-spec refresh with upgraded components
M30/M52 engines are no longer produced new; reconditioned units are the standard

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 728i's E23/E32 M30 and E38 M52 eras (1977–2001). Different generations command different pricing — see the Era Map above for variant-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 728i engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). Both the M30 and M52 are relatively straightforward to work on. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: M30 Head Gasket Failure (E23, E32)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from exhaust, oil in coolant or coolant in oil, misfiring, loss of power.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,800 (head gasket replacement, head skimming if warped)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see signs of head gasket failure, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: Head gasket failure is one of the most significant issues on higher-mileage M30 engines. The M30 engine is very durable if well maintained — the problem is, most aren't. If you notice coolant loss, overheating, or white smoke, have the head gasket tested immediately. Proactive replacement of the cooling system (water pump, thermostat, hoses) can help prevent overheating that causes head gasket failure.

problems.title: M52 VANOS System Failure (E38)
problems.symptoms: Loss of low-end torque, hesitation on acceleration, rough idle, reduced fuel economy, check engine light, rattle from the front of the engine.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect variable valve timing

problems.recommendation: The M52's VANOS system is a well-known weak point. The seals degrade over time, causing a loss of low-end torque and hesitation. The 728i is considered to have an "extremely reliable M52TUB28 engine, detuned by BMW so as not to jeopardise 735i sales". However, VANOS seal replacement is a common maintenance item on higher-mileage cars. If you experience hesitation or loss of power, have the VANOS system inspected.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature fluctuations, sweet smell from engine bay, low coolant warning.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to head gasket damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: Cooling system failures are common across both M30 and M52 engines. Aged plastics and rubber components are a particular concern on the E38. If you notice coolant loss or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system pressure-tested immediately. Proactive replacement of the water pump, thermostat and hoses at 100,000-mile intervals is recommended preventative maintenance.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve cover or oil pan, low oil level warning.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (valve cover gasket or oil pan gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are among the most common issues on both M30 and M52 engines. The M30 suffers from valve cover and oil pan gasket leaks. The M52 also has well-documented oil leak issues. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have it inspected promptly. Regular inspections can catch these before they become major issues.

problems.title: M52 Nikasil Cylinder Liner Issues (E38, Early Cars)
problems.symptoms: Loss of compression, misfiring, rough running, increased oil consumption, eventual engine failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you suspect Nikasil failure, have it inspected immediately

problems.recommendation: Early M52 engines used Nikasil-coated cylinder liners, which could be damaged by high-sulphur fuel. BMW later switched to Alusil liners, which are much more durable. Most affected engines were replaced under warranty, but it's worth checking the engine number on any E38 728i. The M52 engine is generally considered reliable, but this is a known issue on early cars.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 728i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* Cooling system in good order (recent water pump, thermostat, hoses)
* E38: VANOS seals replaced or functioning correctly
* E38: Nikasil issue addressed (or later Alusil engine fitted)
* No signs of head gasket failure (no coolant loss, no overheating, no white smoke)
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these engines are durable but unforgiving of neglect
* Signs of head gasket failure — expensive repair ahead
* E38 with Nikasil engine and no proof of replacement — potential catastrophic failure
* Any overheating history — potential head warping or gasket damage
* Excessive oil consumption or leaks — may indicate worn piston rings or valve seals

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 728i is a true classic — the original 7 Series flagship that set the standard for luxury saloons. The M30 'big six' in the E23 and E32 is one of BMW's most durable engines, while the M52 in the E38 offers modern refinement with generally solid reliability. Across all generations, the same rules apply: regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance, and attention to age-related component wear are essential. Buy on service history, maintain on a regular schedule, and this is one of the most rewarding classic BMWs you can own.

cta.label: → Compare 728i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 728i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattles (timing chain tensioner on M30, VANOS on M52)
* Service History — Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are essential
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates cooling system issues
* Head Gasket — Any signs of coolant loss, overheating, or white smoke? (M30)
* VANOS Operation — Any hesitation or loss of low-end torque? (M52, E38)
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover and oil pan
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Cooling, VANOS, or emissions codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to fuel system or age-related component wear)
